title: Chapter 12 — Building a Reusable Prompt Library
CHAPTER 12 —
BUILDING A REUSABLE PROMPT LIBRARY

Chapter 12 — Building a Reusable Prompt — Three weeks later you need the same thing. You start from scratch. The second attempt takes fifteen minutes and produces

beats.text: Somewhere in your chat history there is a prompt that actually worked. You spent twenty minutes refining it, added the right constraints, provided an example, and Claude produced exactly what you needed. You closed the tab. You moved on. Three weeks later you need the same thing. You start from scratch. The second attempt takes fifteen minutes and produces something worse. The version that worked is buried under hundreds of messages you will never find again.

segments.label: 01 / 11 · HOOK

title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS
YOU DO.
segments.body_html:
·Convert a successful one-off prompt into a reusable prompt card with all the components a colleague (or your future self
·Organize a personal or team prompt library that is easy to search and update
·Recognize when a prompt is going stale and knows what to do about it
·Apply a maintenance discipline so the library grows more useful rather than more cluttered

beats.text: By the end of this chapter you will be able to: Convert a successful one-off prompt into a reusable prompt card with all the components a colleague (or your future self) needs to use it Organize a personal or team prompt library that is easy to search and update Recognize when a prompt is going stale and knows what to do about it Apply a maintenance discipline so the library grows more useful rather than more cluttered

segments.label: 02 / 11 · WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

title: WHY PROMPTS
GO STALE.
segments.body_html: The task drifts.→The model changes.→The prompt was never generalized.

beats.text: A prompt card is only as good as its last test. Prompts fail to stay useful for three reasons. The task drifts. Your document format changed. The audience shifted. You now write for a different publication. The original prompt still works in the narrow sense , Claude responds , but the output no longer fits.

segments.label: 03 / 11 · WHY PROMPTS GO STALE

title: THE PROMPT
CARD FORMAT.
segments.body_html:
·Raw meeting notes (paste as plain text below the prompt)
·Meeting date and participants (state in the prompt)
·Output must not exceed 300 words
·Action items must include an assigned person and a due date if stated in the notes
·If no due date was stated, write "due date not stated" rather than inferring one

beats.text: The fundamental unit of a prompt library is the prompt card. A prompt card is not just the prompt text. It is the prompt text plus the information needed to use it correctly and maintain it over time. A complete prompt card includes eight fields.

segments.label: 04 / 11 · THE PROMPT CARD FORMAT

title: BEFORE AND AFTER:
PROMOTING A ONE-OFF PROMPT
segments.body_html:
·Key decisions (bulleted list, 3–5 items)
Action items (tableAction | Owner | Due Date)
·Maximum 300 words
If a due date was not stated in the notes, write "due date not stated"do not estimate
·Do not include decisions or actions not present in the notes

beats.text: This is the kind of prompt a person writes when they need something right now. Here are some meeting notes. Can you pull out the action items?

segments.label: 05 / 11 · BEFORE AND AFTER: PROMOTING A

title: LIBRARY ORGANIZATION
segments.body_html:
·A naming convention. Use a short prefix that indicates the domain (MEET, WRITE, DATA, RESEARCH, CODE) followed by a numb
·An index. A single file that lists every card by ID and purpose. When you are looking for a prompt, you search the index
·A tested flag. Each card should note when it was last tested and whether it is currently working. A card that has not be

beats.text: A prompt library does not need to be complicated. A folder of text files works. A shared document works. What matters is that prompts are findable when you need them. A naming convention. Use a short prefix that indicates the domain (MEET, WRITE, DATA, RESEARCH, CODE) followed by a number and a brief descriptor. WRITE-04-abstract-draft is findable. "Untitled Prompt 7" is not.

segments.label: 06 / 11 · LIBRARY ORGANIZATION

title: THE HUMAN
GATE.
segments.body_html:
A prompt library is not a vending machine. Every card in the library was validated by a human who checked the output, no

The Human Gate — The discipline of building a library is itself a quality check. When you try to write the review criteria field and find

beats.text: A prompt library is not a vending machine. Every card in the library was validated by a human who checked the output, noted what worked, and documented the failure modes. The library preserves that judgment , it does not replace it. The discipline of building a library is itself a quality check. When you try to write the review criteria field and find you cannot articulate what good output looks like, that is useful information.

segments.label: 07 / 11 · THE HUMAN GATE

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Saving prompts without testing them as templates.→Skipping the review criteria field.→Building a library but never pruning it.→Not versioning.→Library without an index.

beats.text: Saving prompts without testing them as templates. The original prompt worked on specific material with specific context the user held in their head. Saving that prompt as a card without generalizing it produces a card that only works for one person on one task. Skipping the review criteria field. Review criteria are what allow another person , or your future self , to judge the output correctly. Without them, the card transfers the prompt but not the standard.

segments.label: 08 / 11 · COMMON MISTAKES

title: CLOSING:
FROM ASSET TO PRACTICE
segments.body_html:
The pattern libraries and style guides that competent practitioners maintained before AI are the ancestors of the prompt

Closing: From Asset to Practice — The prompt library is the same discipline in a new medium. It says: this specification worked, this review passed, this

beats.text: The pattern libraries and style guides that competent practitioners maintained before AI are the ancestors of the prompt library. A senior editor's annotation of what makes a good abstract, a researcher's checklist for a literature summary, a project manager's template for a meeting debrief , these existed because experienced people recognized that good work has a repeatable structure worth preserving. The prompt library is the same discipline in a new medium. It says: this specification worked, this review passed, this constraint was necessary. Record it. Maintain it.

segments.label: 09 / 11 · CLOSING: FROM ASSET TO PRACTIC
